module.title: Mastering
module.description: Understand what mastering does, how to prepare a mix for it, and how to build a master bus chain that makes your track competitive on streaming platforms — without destroying the dynamics that make it worth listening to.

lessons.title: What Mastering Is (and Isn't)
lessons.body:
Mastering is the final processing stage before a track is distributed. It prepares the mix for the widest possible range of playback environments — streaming services, vinyl, club systems, phones, car stereos — and ensures it sits at a consistent, competitive loudness relative to other tracks on the same platform.

Mastering is widely misunderstood. Producers often expect it to fix problems in the mix, or to add 'something' that was missing. Understanding what mastering can and cannot do prevents wasted effort and sets realistic expectations.

## What Mastering Can Do

**Optimise loudness**: Bring the track to a loudness level appropriate for streaming distribution (typically around −14 LUFS integrated) using a combination of compression and limiting.

**Correct broad tonal balance**: If a mix is slightly dark, slightly harsh, or slightly heavy in the low-mids, mastering EQ can make small corrective adjustments to the overall spectral balance.

**Enhance stereo width**: Mid-side processing on the master can subtly widen the stereo image or clean up phase issues in the low end.

**Ensure consistency across an album**: When mastering a collection of tracks, the mastering stage aligns loudness, tonal character, and spacing between tracks for a cohesive listening experience.

**Protect against clipping**: The master limiter ensures no sample exceeds the digital ceiling during distribution and format conversion.

## What Mastering Cannot Do

**Fix a bad mix**: Mastering applies processing to the full stereo mix — two channels of audio. It cannot reach inside that stereo file and separately adjust the kick, the bass, or the vocals. A kick that is too quiet in the mix is too quiet in the master; the only fix is returning to the mix.

**Add elements that are not there**: If the mix lacks excitement, punch, or clarity, mastering cannot create those qualities from nothing. It can only enhance what is present.

**Correct phase problems**: If tracks in the mix are cancelling each other due to phase issues, the master receives that cancellation baked into the stereo file. Mastering cannot undo it.

**Make a bad song sound good**: Mastering improves the technical presentation of a mix. It does not improve the composition, arrangement, or performances.

## The Mix Is the Foundation

The most important mastering principle: **a great mix needs minimal mastering**. A well-gain-staged, well-balanced mix with clean low end, controlled dynamics, and proper headroom should reach near-competitive loudness with just a limiter. When a master bus chain requires aggressive EQ, heavy compression, and hard limiting to sound acceptable, the mix has problems that should be solved in the mix.

**The headroom rule**: Leave −3 to −6 dBFS of headroom on the master before any limiting. This gives the mastering chain room to work without clipping on input. If your master peaks at −1 dBFS before the limiter, the limiter is doing emergency clipping prevention rather than musical loudness shaping.

## Self-Mastering vs Professional Mastering

For most independent producers releasing to streaming platforms, self-mastering is entirely viable if the mix is solid. The tools available in FL Studio and free plugins are sufficient to produce master-ready audio.

Professional mastering is valuable when:
- The tracks will be released on vinyl (requires specific EQ and phase corrections)
- An album needs consistency across many tracks with different mixes
- The mix was done on inaccurate monitors and needs an objective outside listen
- The track is for sync licensing or broadcast and must meet specific technical standards

For most electronic music and hip-hop released digitally, a careful self-master on accurate headphones or monitors is the practical choice.
keyTakeaways: Mastering cannot fix mix problems — it processes the full stereo file as two channels and cannot reach inside to adjust individual elements. A bad mix produces a bad master regardless of how much processing is applied.
Leave −3 to −6 dBFS of headroom on the master output before any limiting — a mix peaking at −1 dBFS has no room for a mastering chain to work musically.

A great mix needs minimal mastering: a well-balanced mix with clean low end and proper headroom should need only a limiter and perhaps one or two small EQ moves to reach competitive streaming loudness.
lessons.tryThis: Export a full mix WAV from your current project without any master bus processing. Open the file in Edison or check the peak level in the export dialog. If the peak is above −3 dBFS, go back to your mix and reduce levels until the pre-master export peaks at −6 dBFS or lower. This is the starting point for mastering — and the exercise often reveals gain staging problems in the mix that you did not notice at mix volume. Fix those problems in the mix before adding any master bus processing.

lessons.title: Preparing Your Mix for Mastering
lessons.body:
The difference between a mix that masters cleanly in 20 minutes and one that requires an hour of troubleshooting is almost always preparation. A properly prepared mix gives the mastering chain predictable, consistent input and requires only enhancement rather than correction. This lesson covers every preparation step before you touch the master bus.

## Step 1: Remove All Master Bus Processing

Before preparing a mix for mastering, remove or bypass everything on the master bus — limiters, compressors, EQ, saturators. You need to hear the raw mix to evaluate it accurately and give yourself headroom for the mastering chain.

Common mistake: mixing with a limiter on the master to achieve a loud, competitive sound while mixing. This masks problems, encourages poor gain staging, and leaves no headroom for the actual mastering stage. Mix without a master limiter. Add it only at the mastering stage.

## Step 2: Gain Staging Audit

With master bus processing removed, check that the mix peaks at −3 to −6 dBFS on the master meter. Play through the loudest section of the track:

- If the master peaks above −3 dBFS: reduce levels across the mix. The quickest fix is to lower the master fader or add −3 dB of output gain on the master bus. The better fix is to revisit individual track and bus levels.
- If the master peaks below −12 dBFS: the mix is very quiet. Check if tracks are under-levelled or if there is an issue with bus routing.

Target: the loudest section of the track peaks at approximately −4 to −6 dBFS with no master bus processing.

## Step 3: Low-End Mono Check

Add Fruity Stereo Enhancer to the master with Width set to 0 (mono). Play the full mix:

- Does the kick still hit clearly?
- Does the bass stay audible and present?
- Do any elements disappear completely?

Elements disappearing in mono indicate phase cancellation — two signals that are out of phase with each other and cancel when summed to mono. Common causes: excessive stereo chorus or flanger on bass, out-of-phase sample layering, or heavy stereo width on low-frequency content.

Fix mono-failing elements in the mix before mastering. The master cannot repair phase cancellation.

## Step 4: Reference Check

Load a commercial reference track at matched loudness and compare your mix to it:

- **Low end**: Is your kick and bass weight in the same range as the reference?
- **Mids**: Does your mix have similar presence and clarity in the 500 Hz–3 kHz range?
- **Highs**: Are your highs equally open, or dull, or harsh compared to the reference?
- **Overall energy**: Does the track feel as alive and dynamic as the reference?

Note specific differences — these are the targets for your mastering EQ and dynamics moves.

## Step 5: Check the Arrangement Tails

Play the very end of the track past the last note. Ensure:
- No reverb tails are cut off abruptly by the export region ending
- No automation clips leave parameters at unexpected values at the end
- The final decay fades to silence cleanly — no pops, clicks, or audio artefacts

Extend your export region by 2–4 bars beyond the last audible content to capture full reverb and delay tails.

## Step 6: Export the Pre-Master Mix

Export the fully prepared mix — without master bus processing — as a 32-bit float WAV at the project's sample rate (usually 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz). The 32-bit float format preserves full dynamic range even at levels above 0 dBFS internally, giving maximum quality for the mastering stage.

In FL Studio: **File → Export → WAV**, set quality to **32 Bit float**, ensure the export region covers the full track plus tail. This is the file you master.

## Working on Your Own Mix vs a Separate Master Session

For self-mastering, you can master directly in the same FL Studio project by processing the master bus after mixing. However, mastering in a separate session (importing the exported WAV into a new project) has advantages:

- Fresh ears without the memory of mixing decisions influencing judgement
- A clean signal path focused entirely on the master bus chain
- Easier to compare before and after at the full-track level
keyTakeaways: Remove all master bus processing before preparing the mix — mixing through a limiter masks gain staging problems and leaves no headroom for mastering. Mix raw, master separately.
Check the mix in mono before mastering: elements that disappear indicate phase cancellation that cannot be fixed at the mastering stage and must be addressed in the mix.
Export the pre-master mix as 32-bit float WAV — this format preserves full dynamic range regardless of levels, giving the mastering chain maximum quality to work with.
lessons.tryThis: Bypass everything on your master bus. Play the loudest section of your track and note the peak level on the master meter. If it exceeds −3 dBFS, reduce your master fader until the peak sits between −4 and −6 dBFS. Now add Fruity Stereo Enhancer at Width 0 and play the full mix in mono — note which elements feel weaker or disappear. Return those elements to their individual tracks and check for phase issues (out-of-phase layers, excessive stereo effects on bass). Fix any problems before continuing. This preparation step alone resolves the majority of mastering-stage problems.

lessons.title: Master Bus EQ and Stereo Enhancement
lessons.body:
Master bus EQ and stereo enhancement are the tonal and spatial tools of mastering. Applied correctly, they make small, targeted improvements that shift the overall character of the mix — brightening a dark master, clearing low-end mud, widening a narrow image. Applied incorrectly, they destabilise a balanced mix and introduce problems that were not there before.

The guiding principle: every move should be gentle. Master bus EQ deals in fractions and single decibels. If you find yourself reaching for a 6 dB cut on the master, the problem belongs in the mix.

## Master Bus EQ

Use Parametric EQ 2 on the master bus for broad tonal corrections. The corrections available at the mastering stage are limited to what affects the full mix simultaneously — they work well for global tonal balance but cannot fix individual element problems.

### Low-End Management

**High-pass the master at 20–30 Hz**: Sub-sonic content below 20 Hz is inaudible but takes up headroom and can overload limiters unpredictably. A gentle high-pass at 20–30 Hz removes it cleanly.

**Low shelf adjustment**: If the mix sounds slightly heavy (too much weight below 150 Hz), a gentle low shelf cut (−1 to −2 dB at 80–100 Hz) lightens the overall mass without removing the kick and bass fundamentals. If the mix sounds thin, a +1 to +2 dB low shelf boost adds global weight.

### Mid-Range Clarity

**Low-mid cut (250–400 Hz)**: The most common mastering EQ move. Most mixes accumulate a slight cloudiness in this range from multiple instruments stacking their low harmonics. A gentle bell cut (−1 to −2 dB, wide Q of 0.8–1.0) at 300 Hz often immediately clarifies the mix without removing warmth.

**Upper-mid presence (2–5 kHz)**: If the reference comparison revealed your mix lacks presence or sounds slightly dull compared to a reference, a gentle bell boost here (+0.5 to +1.5 dB, wide Q) adds forward definition to the full mix.

### High-End Air

**High shelf boost (8–16 kHz)**: The classic mastering 'air shelf.' A gentle high shelf (+1 to +2 dB at 10–12 kHz) opens the mix and adds perceived brightness and spaciousness. Set the slope gently — a steep shelf can add harshness. +1.5 dB at 12 kHz with a gentle slope is a common starting point.

**High-pass steeply if there is hiss**: If the mix has noise floor hiss from samples or recordings, a low-pass filter at 18–20 kHz removes inaudible hiss without affecting the mix character.

### Master Bus EQ Amounts — The Reference

Compare every master bus EQ move against the reference at matched loudness. The EQ should move the mix *toward* the reference character — not past it, and not in a direction that makes your track sound like the reference rather than itself.

## Mid-Side EQ on the Master

As covered in Module 9, mid-side EQ processes the centre and stereo width independently:

**Side channel high-pass at 100–120 Hz** (the most universally recommended mastering move): Forces sub-bass and bass to mono, tightening the low end and improving playback on subwoofers and mono club systems.

**Side channel high-shelf boost at 10–12 kHz, +1 to +2 dB**: Brightens the stereo width (cymbals, pads, reverb tails) without adding brightness to the centred kick, bass, and lead.

**Side channel low-mid cut at 250–350 Hz, −1 to −2 dB**: Cleans up blurry, phase-inconsistent stereo width in the low-mids without affecting the warm, centred bass content.

## Stereo Enhancement

Fruity Stereo Enhancer on the master bus controls the overall stereo width of the mixed output:

**Gentle width increase (Width knob to 1.1–1.2)**: Slightly expands the stereo image — pads and reverb tails spread slightly wider, the mix feels more open. Always verify in mono after widening — if elements disappear or the low end loses definition, reduce the width.

**Avoid heavy widening on the master**: Width above 1.4 introduces significant phase cancellation in mono. It is nearly always better to widen individual elements in the mix than to widen the full master.

## A Minimal Master Bus EQ Chain

This chain covers the most common mastering EQ tasks in one organised set of moves:

1. **Parametric EQ 2 (Mid mode)**: High-pass at 25 Hz. Bell at 300 Hz: −1.5 dB, Q 0.9. Bell at 3 kHz: +0.8 dB, Q 1.0. High shelf at 12 kHz: +1.5 dB.
2. **Parametric EQ 2 (Side mode)**: High-pass at 120 Hz. High shelf at 10 kHz: +1.5 dB.
3. **Fruity Stereo Enhancer**: Width 1.05–1.1 (subtle).

These are starting points. Apply only the moves that are genuinely needed based on the reference comparison — not all of them on every track.
keyTakeaways: Master bus EQ moves should be gentle — +1 to +2 dB maximum for tonal shaping. If a fix requires more than 3 dB on the master bus, the problem belongs in the mix, not at the mastering stage.

The side channel high-pass at 100–120 Hz is the single most universally applied mastering move: forcing sub-bass content to mono tightens the low end and ensures correct playback on mono club systems and subwoofers.

Apply only the EQ moves that close the gap identified in the reference comparison — not a full template of moves regardless of what the mix needs. A mix that already sounds balanced may need no master EQ at all.
lessons.tryThis: Set up your master bus M/S EQ using two instances of Parametric EQ 2 (one in Mid mode, one in Side mode). On the Side instance, apply a high-pass at 120 Hz. Play the full mix and toggle the Side high-pass on and off — the difference is subtle but the low end should feel tighter and more defined with it active. Now on the Mid instance, add a bell at 300 Hz set to −1.5 dB with Q of 0.9. Toggle it in and out. If the mix sounds clearer without the cloudiness it had before, keep it. If you cannot hear a difference, remove it — that move is not needed on this mix.

lessons.title: Master Bus Compression and Limiting
lessons.body:
The master bus dynamics chain has two jobs: glue and protection. The compressor provides glue — making the full mix feel cohesive and energetic. The limiter provides protection — ensuring no sample exceeds the digital ceiling and bringing the track to competitive loudness. They work together in sequence, and each has a clearly defined role.

## The Master Bus Chain Order

The standard mastering dynamics chain order:

**EQ → Compressor → EQ (optional) → Limiter**

- EQ first: shape the tonal balance before compression reacts to it
- Compressor: glue and cohesion, gentle settings
- Second EQ (optional): compensate for any tonal shift the compressor introduced
- Limiter: absolute ceiling and loudness — last in the chain, always

Never put the limiter before the compressor. The limiter sets the ceiling; the compressor needs to work on the full dynamic range of the mix to function correctly.

## Master Bus Compression

As covered in Module 10 Lesson 4 (Bus Compression and Glue), master bus compression uses gentle settings to make the mix breathe as a unified whole:

**Starting settings for master bus compression (Fruity Limiter, COMP mode)**:
- Ratio: 1.5:1 to 2:1
- Attack: 30–60 ms (slow — lets transients through, compresses sustained content)
- Release: 200–400 ms (breathes with the tempo)
- Threshold: Set for 1–3 dB of gain reduction on the loudest peaks
- Make-up gain: compensate to match bypass level for honest A/B

That is it. If you are compressing more than 3–4 dB on the master bus at these ratios, pull the threshold back and address the dynamic inconsistency in the mix.

### The Toggle Test for Master Compression

Toggle the master compressor on and off while the full track plays. With the compressor on:
- The track should feel slightly more cohesive, forward, and energetic
- The loudest and quietest sections should feel more evenly levelled
- No obvious pumping, breathing artefacts, or dulled transients

If the only audible difference is that the compressed version sounds louder (without make-up gain compensation), the compressor is not doing musical work — it is just a gain stage. Level-match and compare again.

## The Master Limiter

The limiter is the final plugin in the master bus chain. Its two settings that matter most:

**Ceiling**: Set to −0.3 dBFS or −1.0 dBFS. Never 0 dBFS — inter-sample peaks and format conversion can push the actual audio level above 0 even if the digital sample peak reads 0. A −0.3 dBFS ceiling provides safety.

**Input gain (drive into the limiter)**: This is your loudness control. Increase it to drive more signal into the limiter, which catches more peaks and allows a higher average level (higher LUFS). Decrease it for a more dynamic, less limited master.

**Release on the limiter**: Set to 300–500 ms for transparent limiting. Faster release can cause distortion; slower release can dull dynamics. Fruity Limiter's LIMIT tab handles this automatically in most settings.

## Finding the Loudness Sweet Spot

The relationship between limiting drive and sound quality:

**Drive +2 dB** (light limiting): Clean, dynamic master. Kick punches hard, transients are intact. LUFS reads around −16 to −14. Platforms like Spotify and Apple Music will not turn it down.

**Drive +4–6 dB** (moderate limiting): More forward and energetic. Some transient softening. LUFS around −12 to −10. Sounds loud and competitive without obvious distortion.

**Drive +8+ dB** (heavy limiting): Flat, fatiguing. Transients are gone. The mix sounds loud for 15 seconds then becomes exhausting. LUFS above −8. Streaming platforms turn it down to −14, eliminating any loudness advantage entirely.

The sweet spot for streaming is typically +3 to +5 dB of input drive into the limiter, targeting −14 to −12 LUFS integrated. Beyond this, loudness gains are offset by dynamic losses.

## Checking for Limiter Distortion

Heavy limiting on bass-heavy material causes inter-sample distortion — the 808 or kick sub creates such large peaks that the limiter clips between samples. Signs:

- The bass or 808 sounds slightly fuzzy or distorted at higher drive settings
- The low end loses definition and punch
- The gain reduction meter shows constant 6+ dB reduction on bass hits

Fix: reduce input drive until the distortion clears. If you cannot reach target LUFS without distortion, address the low-end peak levels in the mix (gentle 808 compression or EQ) before returning to the master.

## The Final A/B

Before exporting the master, A/B the full processed master against your best reference track at matched loudness. The master should feel competitive — similar energy, similar tonal balance, similar dynamics — without sounding like a copy of the reference. If the master sounds noticeably weaker or flatter than the reference at the same LUFS level, the issue is in the mix.
keyTakeaways: The master bus chain order is always EQ → Compressor → Limiter (never limiter before compressor) — the compressor needs the full dynamic range of the mix to function as glue, and the limiter sets the final ceiling last.

Master bus compression targets 1–3 dB of gain reduction at 1.5:1–2:1 ratio with slow attack (30–60 ms) and tempo-matched release — if the toggle test reveals only a loudness difference rather than cohesion, level-match and compare again.

The loudness sweet spot for streaming is +3 to +5 dB of limiter input drive targeting −14 to −12 LUFS — driving harder eliminates dynamic contrast, and streaming platforms normalise heavily limited tracks back down anyway.
lessons.tryThis: Build the full master bus chain in order: Parametric EQ 2 (for any EQ moves from Lesson 3) → Fruity Limiter in COMP mode (ratio 2:1, attack 40 ms, release 300 ms, threshold for 2 dB reduction) → Fruity Limiter in LIMIT mode (ceiling −0.3 dBFS). Slowly increase the LIMIT tab input gain until the gain reduction meter shows 2–4 dB on the loudest hits. Export a 30-second section and note the perceived loudness. Push the drive until the gain reduction shows 8+ dB and export again. Compare the two exports on a phone speaker — the heavy version should sound louder for about 10 seconds before becoming fatiguing. The lighter version should feel more musical on repeated listens.
